title: Anthropic models restored, oil analysts cut forecasts, gold at 8-month low
subhead: The US lifted export controls on Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on July 1, oil analysts slashed price forecasts as Hormuz reopening looms, and gold hit an eight-month low as Fed rate hike bets hardened after the June FOMC dot plot.

sections.heading: Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 restored after export control reversal
sections.body:
The US Department of Commerce lifted export controls on Anthropic's Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models on June 30, ending a two-and-a-half-week standoff that had cut off all foreign nationals — including Anthropic's own non-US employees — from both models. Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick announced the decision, saying the department had 'worked closely with Anthropic to analyze and approve Fable 5.' Anthropic confirmed it would begin restoring access on July 1.

The original June 12 order cited national security after a security researcher at Amazon reported a jailbreak that could bypass Fable 5's guardrails. Access to Mythos 5 was partially restored on June 26 for over 100 vetted US organizations and federal agencies through Anthropic's Glasswing cybersecurity program. The full lifting now returns both models to general availability worldwide. Neither Anthropic nor Commerce detailed what guardrail changes, if any, were made to satisfy the review.

French politician Gabriel Attal had called the shutdown proof that 'the AI war has already begun,' comparing Anthropic's chokepoint to the Strait of Hormuz. Tech executives and investors argued the restrictions handed runway to Chinese open-source developers. Anthropic said Fable 5 would be included for up to 50% of weekly usage limits through July 7 for Pro, Max, Team, and select enterprise users, and would be re-enabled on AWS, Google Cloud, and Microsoft Foundry as soon as possible.
sections.whyItMatters: The 18-day shutdown was the most aggressive use of export controls on an AI model to date. The reversal suggests the administration concluded the competitive cost of sidelining its most capable model — while Chinese alternatives advanced — outweighed the security risk. The episode sets a precedent for how Washington treats frontier AI: intervene hard, then back off when the economic and strategic costs become clear.

sections.heading: Oil analysts cut forecasts as Hormuz reopening shifts supply outlook
sections.body:
A Reuters poll published June 30 found that analysts cut their 2026 oil price forecasts for the first time since the Iran war began, after five straight months of increases. The shift comes as the US-Iran 14-point memorandum of understanding, signed June 17 at Versailles, sets a framework for reopening the Strait of Hormuz — though the details remain contested.

The IEA's June 17 monthly report slashed its 2026 demand outlook by 700,000 barrels per day to just 1.1 mb/d year-over-year growth, after deliveries plunged by 5 mb/d in Q2. Global supply slumped to 94.5 mb/d in May — 13.6 mb/d below pre-war levels. However, the IEA projected supply surging by roughly 8 mb/d to 110 mb/d next year, creating a 'significant overhang' as demand recovers only modestly to 105.3 mb/d in 2027.

Brent crude was last at $78.44 on June 17, down nearly 4% on deal optimism, while WTI fell to $75.18. Fitch Ratings projects Brent averaging $100-110/bbl through July, dropping to $80 in August and around $70 from September. Rabobank cut its Q3 forecast to $103 and Q4 to $93, also lowering 2027 to $85/bbl. Chinese seaborne crude imports fell to 6.7 mb/d in May — the lowest in a decade — as refiners cut throughput to 13.1 mb/d, down 1.8 mb/d year-on-year.

Clearing the Strait remains the operative bottleneck. Kpler estimates 500 commercial vessels remain trapped in the Persian Gulf. Mine clearance could take six months, with full production recovery requiring an additional three months. Iran has demanded tolls for passage, while Trump claimed the deal involves 'toll free' transit — a discrepancy the 60-day negotiation period has yet to resolve.
sections.whyItMatters: The market is transitioning from acute supply shock to prospective glut, but the timing mismatch is real: inventories have drawn at a record 3.8 mb/d pace since the war began, and the IEA warns global stocks could hit historic lows before surpluses emerge. The gap between the deal on paper and tankers moving freely through Hormuz is measured in months, not days.

sections.heading: Gold hits eight-month low as Fed rate hike bets dominate
sections.body:
Spot gold fell 0.7% to $3,981.50/oz on July 1, an eight-month low, capping the steepest quarterly decline since 2013 at roughly 14% for Q2. US gold futures dropped 1.1% to $3,994.32. Silver slid 1.3% to $57.80, down 22% for the quarter, while platinum fell 0.4% to $1,548, off 21%.

The sell-off is driven by a decisive shift in Fed expectations. The June FOMC voted 12-0 to hold the federal funds rate at 3.50%-3.75% for the fourth consecutive meeting. But the updated dot plot showed nearly half of policymakers now support at least one rate hike by year-end, with year-end projections raised to 3.6%-4.1%. CME FedWatch data confirms the market pricing one 25bp hike by October 2026.

Minneapolis Fed President Neel Kashkari said he penciled in one rate hike for 2026. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh promised the central bank would 'deliver on price stability' at his first meeting. Persistent inflation concerns — from elevated energy costs during the Iran war to rising semiconductor prices and Apple's June price increases — have replaced recession fears as the dominant policy driver.
sections.whyItMatters: Gold's 14% quarterly drop signals a regime change in market pricing: the Iran deal has removed the geopolitical premium even as the Fed reconstitutes a hawkish stance. The combination of easing oil prices and a potential rate hike is doubly negative for gold — higher real yields increase the opportunity cost while reducing safe-haven demand.

sections.heading: UK seizes Russian shadow fleet tanker in English Channel
sections.body:
Royal Marine Commandos and National Crime Agency officers boarded the Russian oil tanker Smyrtos in a six-hour operation in the English Channel on June 14 — the first time the UK has seized a shadow fleet vessel in its own waters. The operation was supported by the Royal Air Force, and the tanker is now being held off the southern coast of England.

Prime Minister Keir Starmer publicly confirmed the seizure, framing it as a direct move to disrupt Russia's network of aging tankers used to circumvent Western oil sanctions. The legal basis came from the UK government's March 26 authorization for law enforcement to intercept and detain sanctioned Russian vessels in or near British waters. The UK's sanctions list now covers over 600 shadow fleet and Russian LNG vessels.

The UK cooperated with France in a separate shadow fleet seizure on June 1 before executing its own operation independently. Starmer's public attribution of the action is notable — leaders rarely attach their names to maritime enforcement, signaling that shadow fleet disruption is now a declared policy priority for London.
sections.whyItMatters: The Smyrtos seizure marks an escalation from passive sanctions enforcement to active interdiction in European waters. Combined with the UK's 600+ vessel sanctions list, it signals that shadow fleet disruption is shifting from a regulatory exercise to a naval operation — one that could trigger diplomatic friction with Russia and complicate insurance and shipping markets.

sections.heading: Geely's Lotus EVs set to enter Canada under Carney-Xi tariff deal
sections.body:
China's Geely Holding Group will ship its first Lotus-brand electric vehicles to Canada in July under the trade agreement reached between Prime Minister Mark Carney and Chinese President Xi Jinping, China's ambassador to Canada Wang Di told Reuters on June 26.

The deal allows up to 49,000 Chinese EVs annually into Canada at reduced tariff rates — a targeted carve-out that stands in sharp contrast to the US, which maintains a 100% tariff on Chinese EVs. The Lotus shipments will be the first Chinese-owned, Chinese-built cars to enter the Canadian market under the new framework. More Chinese brands are expected to follow.

Carney pitched the EV quota concept to Trump at the G7 summit, according to Canadian officials. The arrangement is designed to give Canada access to affordable EVs for its climate targets while maintaining a volume cap that protects domestic auto manufacturing — a balancing act that Washington has so far declined to attempt.
sections.whyItMatters: Canada is carving out a middle path between the US approach (effectively banning Chinese EVs) and the EU approach (tariffs with no quota). The 49,000-unit cap gives Ottawa a controlled experiment in integrating Chinese EVs without swamping domestic producers. If it works, it becomes a model; if Chinese brands flood the quota and undercut local prices, it becomes a cautionary tale for other Western economies.

sections.heading: Singapore's AI-driven export surge hits 22-year high
sections.body:
Singapore's non-oil domestic exports surged 38.4% year-on-year in May, the highest jump since December 2003, according to Enterprise Singapore data released June 17. Electronics NODX rocketed 94.8%, driven by integrated circuits (up 80.9%), disk media products (up 227.8%), and PCs. NODX to Taiwan alone surged 135.2%, reflecting Singapore's upstream role in the AI chip supply chain.

The figures defied the Gulf War shock. Maybank economists upgraded their 2026 NODX growth forecast to 15% from 4.5% and raised GDP expectations to 4.2% (above the official 2-4% range). DBS economist Chua Han Teng noted that Singapore's export cycle is now tightly correlated with AI capex flows from Taiwan and South Korea.

Non-electronics NODX grew 17.7%, driven by pharmaceuticals (up 102.6%) — partly a timing effect as companies front-load shipments ahead of the US 100% tariff on patented drugs taking effect July 31. Singapore, which hosts manufacturing plants for eight of the world's top 10 drugmakers, exports roughly $4 billion in pharmaceuticals to the US annually.
sections.whyItMatters: Singapore's data shows how AI capex is creating a bifurcated trade landscape: electronics exports to Taiwan and South Korea are booming on chip demand, while non-AI trade faces pressure from Middle East disruptions and US tariff walls. The pharma front-loading is a preview of the distortion the US drug tariff will cause once it takes effect.

bottomLine: July 1 marks a pivot point on multiple fronts. Anthropic's Fable 5 returns after 18 days of export controls — the most aggressive AI model restriction to date, now reversed — while oil analysts are finally cutting forecasts as the Hormuz deal shifts the market from supply shock to prospective glut. Gold's eight-month low and 14% quarterly drop reflect the twin removal of geopolitical risk and a newly hawkish Fed: the June dot plot now shows one rate hike by year-end under new Chair Warsh. Singapore's 38.4% export surge confirms AI capex is the dominant trade story, even as Canada's 49,000-unit Chinese EV quota opens a new frontier in Western trade policy toward China. The common thread is recalibration: markets and governments are adjusting from crisis mode to a new normal that looks very different from February.
sources: CNBC, Reuters, AP, Seeking Alpha, EconoTimes, Forbes, Bloomberg, Indian Express, CBS News, Yahoo Finance, Business Standard, Straits Times, CryptoBriefing, Le Monde, Gizmodo, The Deep Dive, Enterprise Singapore, Fitch Ratings, Rabobank, IEA, Kpler, CME FedWatch
